name: Minou
subtitle: The one who made me a cat mom
story: A little old girl with diabetes who gave me the title of cat mom. Without Minou, none of this exists — no Bruce, no Artie, no Milton and Sorbet. She was the beginning of everything.

Managing a diabetic cat is a crash course in paying attention, in learning to read a body that can't tell you what it needs. She gave me that education, and I didn't fully understand what she was teaching me until much later.
lesson: Becoming a cat mom means becoming an advocate. The moment you take on a cat with a chronic condition, you become part of their medical team — whether anyone tells you that or not.

name: Yams
subtitle: Adopted into complexity
story: My first orange boy. Such a sweetie, and he came to us already carrying health issues. The cruelty of Yams's story was the timeline — he declined incredibly quickly, what seemed to be sepsis taking him before we could fight back.
When a cat comes to you already fragile, the system often isn't built for them. The urgency is different. The decisions are faster. The window is smaller.
lesson: Adopted cats with pre-existing conditions deserve the same aggressive advocacy as any other cat. When the window is small, you need to know what questions to ask — and ask them immediately.

name: Murphy
subtitle: Our tripod girl
story: Our adopted tripod girl. Murphy fought on three legs and never slowed down — until breast cancer. We went through chemotherapy with her. It didn't help. She is the hardest lesson this site carries: sometimes you do everything right and it still isn't enough.
Murphy taught us that the goal was never to win every fight. It was to make sure every fight was worth fighting — and that the life in between the battles was full and warm and hers.
lesson: Knowing when to fight and knowing when to change the goal — from cure to comfort, from treatment to quality — is the hardest and most important skill a cat parent can develop.

name: Artie
subtitle: The one the system couldn't answer for
story: My second orange boy. Artie had FIV, hyperthyroidism, and asthma — a complex baseline. Then came neurological symptoms and a dropping body temperature. FIP negative. No cancer detected on cytology. And yet he declined.

The answer never came. We lost him without knowing why. That experience — the gap, the shrug, the unanswered question — is a large part of why this site exists. Artie represents every cat whose owner is told 'we're not sure' and sent home with nothing.
lesson: The undiagnosed cat is one of the loneliest experiences in pet ownership. You deserve a community, a framework, and a set of questions to bring back to your vet — even when the answers aren't clear.

name: Bruce
subtitle: Who this site is named for
story: Bruce was our greatest teacher. For two to three years we managed her CKD through trial and error, vet friction, and a slow accumulation of hard-won knowledge. She showed us that the official guidance often isn't the whole story.

Through Bruce, we learned to push back. To ask a second time when the first answer wasn't enough. To prioritize her happiness over perfectly regulated numbers. To treat her as a whole cat, not a set of lab results.
lesson: Managing CKD is not about chasing perfect bloodwork. It's about keeping your cat comfortable, engaged, and eating — even when that means making choices your vet didn't suggest.

name: Milton
subtitle: Early intervention, on our terms
story: Milton is everything we learned from Bruce, applied early. Managing his CKD proactively — before a crisis, not in response to one. The emphasis is on quality of life, not perfect numbers.
His ongoing care is documented in the Milton & Sorbet updates section. The wins, the experiments, the setbacks, and the small daily choices that add up to a good life.
lesson: Early intervention changes everything. The sooner you start asking questions, the more options you have — and the more time you buy.

name: Sorbet
subtitle: What changes before a crisis begins?
story: Sorbet is the question made real: what happens when you catch kidney disease early and manage it from day one with intention? No panic. No crisis. Just careful, informed, love-driven care.
Like Milton, his story is ongoing. We're figuring it out as we go — and documenting all of it.
lesson: The early-stage CKD cat is an opportunity, not a sentence. What you do before things get bad matters enormously.
